1:
The chapter opens with a solemn re-commissioning, and the witnesses agree that its whole point is to set up a deliberate trial of obedience. Matthew Henry reads the preface as an argument from gratitude: Samuel reminds Saul that "the Lord sent me to anoint thee to be a king," so that the very power he holds is a stewardship — "men's preferment, instead of releasing them from their obedience to God, obliges them so much the more to it." Because this one errand was expressly made the test, Henry observes, the disobedience that followed was all the more aggravated. Adam Clarke makes the same connection tersely: the reminder that God had anointed him "gave him a right to say what immediately follows."

Keil and Delitzsch supply the redemptive-historical frame. Saul had already transgressed once at Gilgal and drawn the threat that his throne would not endure (1 Samuel 13:13–14); this second disobedience will bring his outright rejection. They rehearse the ancient charge: Amalek was the first heathen nation to attack Israel after the Exodus, and did so treacherously, so that God threatened its extermination and had Moses write the sentence down for every generation (Exodus 17:8–16). John Gill only fixes the occasion, suggesting the words were spoken at Gilgal, where prophet and king would meet again.

The emphasis of the schools converges on the authority behind the order. Samuel prefaces it with "Thus saith the Lord of hosts" — the Lord, Gill notes, of the armies of heaven and of Israel — precisely to bind Saul's conscience. John Wesley catches the pastoral undertone in the single word hearken: "Thou hast committed error already; now regain God's favour by thy exact obedience to what he commands." So the witnesses frame the whole narrative before it begins. This is not an arbitrary campaign but the discharge of an old, sworn quarrel; and it is staged, by God's own design, as the proof of whether Israel's first king will submit his crown to the King of kings. Henry's summary governs all that follows: Saul is "advanced to command Israel, but must know that he is a subject to the God of Israel, and must be commanded by him."
2:
Here the witnesses expound the reason annexed to the command, and they dwell on both the majesty of the Speaker and the antiquity of the grievance. John Gill notes that the title "the Lord of hosts" — of the celestial host of angels and of the armies of Israel, indeed of all the armies of earth — is premised "to engage the attention of Saul." Keil and Delitzsch press the force of the verb: when God says "I remember," he "looks upon" the sin and directs his glance toward it, and "when God looks upon a sin... he must punish it according to his own holiness." The word paqadti, they say, points from the outset to the punishment about to be proclaimed.

The grammatical-historical commentators fill in who these Amalekites were. Charles Ellicott describes "a fierce, untameable race of wanderers" who roamed the deserts between southern Judea and Egypt, descended from Esau's grandson Amalek; soon after the Exodus they "cruelly harassed the almost defenceless rear-guard of Israel" and were, at the prayer of Moses, defeated by Joshua and "solemnly doomed to destruction." In Balaam's oracle they stand as "the first of the nations who opposed the Lord's people," and thereafter their hand was "constantly lifted against Israel." Adam Clarke adds the geography — a people of Arabia Petraea on the frontier of Egypt and Palestine — and marvels that the purpose to "blot out Amalek" was "now fulfilled by Saul upwards of four hundred years afterwards."

The heart of the matter, all agree, is the manner of the original assault, spelled out in Deuteronomy 25:18: Amalek "smote the hindmost," the faint and weary, and "feared not God." John Wesley gathers the aggravations most fully. It was barbarous, he says, for when Israel "was newly come out of cruel and long bondage, and was now weak, and weary, and faint, and hungry," Amalek added "affliction to the afflicted" instead of the pity that even nature prompts. And it was "horrid impiety to fight against God himself," lifting the hand "in a manner against the Lord's throne." Wesley also justifies the reach of the sentence across the centuries: the children "continue in their parents' practices," so that in punishing this generation God revenges "those old injuries" upon a people that has never repented of them.
3:
This is the verse where the modern conscience recoils, and the witnesses meet the difficulty of the ban head-on, defending it with different accents. John Gill sets the deed among Israel's standing obligations, citing Kimchi that on entering Canaan three things were required — to appoint a king, to build the sanctuary, and "to blot out the name and memory of Amalek" — and this last work "was reserved for Saul, their first king." Keil and Delitzsch gloss the technical term: Saul is to "ban" everything without reserve, and the catalogue "from man to woman, infant and suckling, ox and sheep" is simply the explanation of that total devotion.

The wesleyan-arminian voices lay the whole weight on the authority of God. Adam Clarke concedes the horror and answers it flatly: "Nothing could justify such an exterminating decree but the absolute authority of God. This was given... The Judge of all the earth doth right." He notes too that the war was stripped of any private motive, for "God commanded that all the property as well as all the people should be destroyed" — it could bring no plunder. John Wesley reasons through the killing of the innocent: it "was not unjust, because God is the supreme Lord of life"; the infants "are born in sin, and therefore liable to God's wrath"; and their death "was rather a mercy than a curse, as being the occasion of preventing their sin and punishment." Even the cattle, "made for man's benefit," suffer with the man "for the instruction of mankind."

Charles Ellicott reframes the act theologically. The Hebrew, he says, is stronger than "utterly destroy": it is "put under the ban" (cherem), and whatever was so devoted "could not be redeemed, but must be slain." "The cup of iniquity in this people was filled up," and Israel "was simply the instrument of destruction used by the Almighty." He forbids us to look here for "materials for the blame or the praise of Israel," since the nation "was not unfrequently used as the visible scourge by which the All-Wise punished hopelessly hardened sinners"; "we might as well find fault with pestilence and famine, or the sword — those awful instruments of Divine justice and... of Divine mercy." Beneath their differences of emphasis the witnesses are one: the command is not Saul's cruelty but God's judgment, long delayed and now discharged, and the trial for Saul is simply whether he will execute it whole.
4:
The muster is a matter of geography and arithmetic, and the witnesses work at both. John Gill takes "gathered the people together" to mean Saul "made them to hear," summoning them by trumpet or by heralds sent through the land, perhaps to Gilgal. Keil and Delitzsch locate the rendezvous: Telaim is "probably the same place as the Telem mentioned in Joshua 15:24," in the eastern Negeb; and they defend the figures — the two hundred thousand foot, drawn from the tribes generally, with ten thousand of Judah besides, "are not too large, for a powerful Bedouin nation... could not possibly be successfully attacked with a small army, but only by raising the whole of the military force of Israel."

The one real disagreement is textual. Adam Clarke lays out the variants: the London Polyglot's Septuagint reads "four hundred thousand companies of Israel and thirty thousand of Judah," the Codex Alexandrinus "ten thousand of each," the Complutensian "two hundred thousand... and ten thousand of Judah," and Josephus agrees with the higher Greek numbers, while "all the other versions" and the manuscripts follow the Hebrew. Charles Ellicott weighs the name itself: with Kimchi he notes that Telaim "signifies lambs," so that "Beth" ("house of") may be understood — not a town at all but, in Dean Payne Smith's phrase, "the place or house of lambs," an open spot where the lambs were gathered from the wilderness pastures.

What the commentators return to is the strange smallness of Judah's contingent. Matthew Henry presses it: Judah's quota "was scandalously short... but a twentieth part of the whole," ten thousand against the other tribes' two hundred thousand, so that "the day of Judah's honour drew near, but had not yet come." He plays on the place-name in the same key — Saul "numbered them in Telaim, which signifies lambs": some read that he "numbered them like lambs," others that he counted them "by the paschal lambs," reckoning ten men to a lamb after the later Jewish manner. The disproportion is quietly ominous, for the tribe that furnished barely a tithe of the army is the tribe from which the true king will come. Whatever the exact tally, the witnesses agree the force was immense — a nation in arms to execute a nation's doom.
5:
A single verse of maneuver draws careful notes on the place, the tactic, and one arresting legend. John Gill takes "a city of Amalek" to be the nearest to Israel's border, and reads "laid wait in the valley" either as an ambush set to intercept the inhabitants or, following the Targum, as Saul's ordering of his army in battle array. Keil and Delitzsch settle the grammar of the disputed verb: wayyareb "does not come from rib, to fight," but is a contracted form of the Hiphil of arab, "to lay wait," so that Saul "placed an ambush in the valley," even as "modern commentators have generally understood it" — though they note that Olshausen questions the reading and Thenius would emend the text.

Adam Clarke presses a small point of translation: the original, he thinks, "should be translated, and Saul came to the city Amalek," the capital "being called by the name of their tribe." Charles Ellicott agrees it is "The city of Amalek... their principal place of arms," and sharpens the topography — "laid wait" should be read "in a torrent bed," the Arabic Wady, "then dry."

It is Ellicott who preserves the verse's outlier, "a strange tradition in the Talmud." The rabbis imagined that Saul's resolve faltered at the very dry torrent-bed: it "called to mind the command of Deuteronomy 21:4 to slay an heifer at a torrent in expiation of a murder," and so, they say, "he determined not to carry out the stern charge of Samuel, but to spare rather than to slay." The legend is fanciful, but the witnesses let it stand as an early attempt to reckon with the mystery that lies ahead — how a king marching under so plain a command could yet turn aside from it. Gill's alternative rendering, that Saul "contended" or gave battle in the valley, only underscores how carefully the narrator marks each stage of the approach, as if to leave Saul without excuse for what follows. On the plain sense they do not divide: Saul has reached the enemy's stronghold, disposed his ambush in the wady, and stands ready to strike. The very precision the commentators recover from the verse — the named city, the dry torrent, the hidden men — becomes, in their hands, the measure of the man. He is meticulous in the arts of war and about to prove slack in the one thing required, obedience; the tactician who lays his ambush so shrewdly will spare the king and the fatlings he was charged to destroy.
6:
Before the sword falls, Saul warns the Kenites to withdraw, and the witnesses read the episode as a lesson in the long memory of God for kindness shown his people. Matthew Henry commends the act as done "prudently and piously, and, it is probable, according to the direction Samuel gave him." The Kenites were "of the family and kindred of Jethro, Moses's father-in-law," tent-dwellers who could easily remove; and Saul "acknowledges the kindness of their ancestors to Israel, when they came out of Egypt." From this Henry draws his favourite comfort: "a good man leaves the divine blessing for an inheritance to his children's children"; "God is not unrighteous to forget the kindnesses shown to his people," but remembers them "at furthest in the great day," saying as it were, "I was hungry, and you gave me meat."

John Gill, Adam Clarke, and Keil and Delitzsch supply the pedigree from Numbers 10:29 and Judges 1:16: Jethro was a Kenite, Hobab guided Israel through the wilderness, and the tribe later dwelt in Judah near Arad. John Wesley traces their wanderings, noting they had once dwelt "in rocks near the Amalekites" (Numbers 24:21) and returned there in troubled times; "some of your progenitors did so," runs the sense of Saul's word, "and for their sakes all of you shall fare the better."

The theological point on which the witnesses converge is God's careful discrimination in judgment. Charles Ellicott observes that this "kindly feeling sprang up soon after the departure from Egypt," so that the Kenites, though nomad Arabs like the Amalekites, "seem to have been ever friendly to the Israelites." Henry and Wesley both draw the same rule: "when destroying judgments are abroad, God will take care to separate between the precious and the vile," and "to hide the meek of the earth in the day of his anger." Hence "it is dangerous being found in the company of God's enemies, and it is our duty and interest to come out from among them, lest we share in their sins and plagues" (Revelation 18:4) — and both quote the Jewish proverb, "Woe to the wicked man, and woe to his neighbour." Gill lets the same providence "clear the righteousness of God": while the ancestors of Amalek are punished, the kindness of the ancestors of the Kenites is rewarded, and mercy and judgment are seen to run in the same family lines.
7:
The battle itself is dispatched in a line, and the witnesses turn chiefly to its geography and, more soberly, to how much of it was left undone. John Gill simply records that Saul "overcame them, and beat them, and slew great numbers." Keil and Delitzsch trace the sweep "from Havilah until thou comest to Shur," the desert bordering Egypt, and set the campaign in its wider scriptural context (compare Genesis 25:18). But they immediately qualify the word "all": Saul "banned all the people with the edge of the sword" only in the sense of "all that fell into the hands of the Israelites." "It follows from the very nature of the case that many escaped," which is why the Amalekites reappear later (1 Samuel 27:8; 1 Samuel 30:1), their "last remnant" not destroyed until the Simeonites finished them on the mountains of Seir in Hezekiah's day (1 Chronicles 4:43).

The grammatical-historical voices debate the exact map. Adam Clarke reports Josephus's reading "from Pelusium in Egypt unto the Red Sea," but corrects it — "Havilah lay eastward from the Red Sea; the Amalekites lay between this and the way to Egypt towards Shur." Charles Ellicott admits "the Havilah here alluded to cannot be now identified," and glosses Shur, which "signifies 'wall,'" as "the wall which crossed the north-east frontier of Egypt, extending from Pelusium," even suggesting that this barrier gave Egypt its name "Mizraim," the enclosed.

John Wesley draws the two threads together, and in doing so quietly names the failure the next verses will expose. Saul smote them "from one end of their country to the other; he smote all that he met with." Yet, Wesley adds, "a great number of them fled away upon the noise of his coming, and secured themselves in other places, till the storm was over." The witnesses thus present a victory that was real but not entire. The command had been for a completed work; the execution was broad but porous, and the survivors who slipped through the net are, for Keil and Wesley alike, the standing proof that a divine charge carried out by halves is not carried out at all — a theme that will now gather to a head over the sparing of Agag.
8:
The verse turns on a single, fateful exception, and the witnesses fasten on the name of the captive king and the meaning of the slaughter. John Gill observes that "Agag" appears to be "a common name of the kings of these people, as Pharaoh was of the Egyptians" (compare the proverb of the king "higher than Agag" in Numbers 24:7). The decisive fact is what Saul did with him: falling into Saul's hands, this king "did not put him to death, as he should have done, but preserved him" — and the reasons for that sparing, Gill says, wait for the next verse to disclose. Against the total ban of "all the people," the survival of the one man at the top is already the crack through which Saul's disobedience shows.

John Wesley reads the destruction of the rest as the long-deferred reckoning of divine justice. Saul "utterly destroyed all the people" — that is, "all whom he found" — and "now they paid dear for the sin of their ancestors." Wesley is careful to add that the Amalekites were not condemned for that ancestral crime alone: "they were themselves guilty of idolatry and numberless sins, for which they deserved to be cut off." Yet when God comes to "reckon with them, he fixes upon this" — their assault on Israel at the Exodus — "as the ground of his quarrel." The generation that fell was a guilty generation in its own right; the ancient injury only named the occasion of a judgment their own wickedness had ripened.

So the two clauses of the verse pull against each other, and the witnesses feel the tension. The people are banned to the last man who could be caught, a nation blotted out for filled-up iniquity; but the king who embodied that iniquity is led away alive. What God had made most sure — that no head of Amalek should survive — is precisely what Saul's mercy, or his vanity, sets aside. The whole tragedy of the chapter is compressed into the contrast: an entire people destroyed in obedience, and one man spared in the disobedience that will cost Saul his crown.
9:
Now the disobedience stands fully exposed, and the witnesses probe both the fact and its motive. Matthew Henry names it bluntly: Saul "did his work by halves." He "spared Agag, because he was a king like himself, and perhaps in hope to get a great ransom for him," and "spared the best of the cattle, and destroyed only the refuse, that was good for little." Henry exposes the perversity of the arrangement: what was destroyed "was in effect sacrificed to the justice of God," and for Saul "to think the torn and the sick, the lame and the lean, good enough for that, while he reserved for his own fields and his own table the firstlings and the fat, was really to honour himself more than God." The whole conduct, he concludes, "was the effect of a proud, rebellious spirit."

John Gill and Charles Ellicott weigh the motive for sparing the king. Gill supposes Saul made the motion himself, moved either "out of respect to him as a king," or by "the comeliness of his person, the height of his stature, and the largeness of his body," as Josephus notes, or to lead him "in triumph in a public show." Ellicott divides the two sins by their two roots: "covetousness seems to have suggested the preservation of the choicest cattle, and pride probably induced the Hebrew king to save Agag alive, that he might show the people his royal captive." Keil and Delitzsch read the same greed in the inventory — Saul and the people "would not utterly destroy" the best of the sheep and oxen, "the fatlings and the lambs and everything good," but destroyed only "everything that was vile and refuse."

John Wesley compresses the indictment into a sentence that catches the calculus of half-obedience exactly: "thus they obeyed God only so far as they could without inconvenience to themselves." That, for the witnesses, is the nerve of the sin. The command had been absolute; Saul kept it wherever keeping it cost nothing and broke it wherever it touched his profit or his pride. He gave God the leavings and kept the prize, and called it obedience. The verse that reports the sparing thus becomes, in their hands, an anatomy of the compromised heart — a will that will serve God up to the point of self-interest, and no further.
10:
The scene shifts from the field to a night of private revelation, and the witnesses treat this brief verse as the hinge on which the whole narrative turns. John Gill identifies the medium: "the word of the Lord," which the Targum calls "the word of prophecy," came to Samuel "in a dream or vision, or by an articulate voice." Charles Ellicott concurs with the simplest reading — the disclosure came "very likely in a dream." Between the triumph at Carmel and the confrontation at Gilgal, God speaks first, and speaks only to his prophet.

Matthew Henry draws out the weight of that secrecy. What follows in the chapter, he says, furnishes "remarkable instances of the strictness of the justice of God and the treachery and deceitfulness of the heart of man"; and the first of these is transacted "in secret," in "what passed between God and Samuel." God "determines Saul's rejection, and acquaints Samuel with it," before ever a word is spoken to the king. The order of events matters to the witnesses: the sentence is settled in heaven and communicated to the prophet while Saul is still parading his victory, ignorant that his cause is already lost. The judgment is not improvised in the heat of the coming quarrel; it is a verdict already reached, which Samuel will be sent to deliver.

There is, too, a tenderness in the way the verse works. God does not confront Saul directly with his doom but entrusts it to the man who had anointed him, the one who loved him and would spend the night pleading for him. The revelation that opens here is thus both a sentence and a summons — a sentence upon Saul's rebellion, and a summons to Samuel to carry a grief he did not choose. The witnesses let the quietness of the verse register its own solemnity: the loud and boastful king at Carmel knows nothing of the word that has just gone out concerning him in the dark, and the reader, admitted to the secret, hears the doom before Saul does. All that remains is for Samuel to rise, as he does at once, and to bring the word of the night into the light of the morning.
11:
God's astonishing word, "It repenteth me that I have set up Saul," presses hard on the doctrine of divine immutability, and the witnesses labour with one mind to keep both truths — that God does not change, and that Scripture may nonetheless speak of his "repenting." John Gill states the rule plainly: this is "not to be understood of any change of mind, counsel, purpose, or decree in God, which is not consistent with his unchangeable nature," but "of a change of dispensation, and outward dealings," spoken "after the manner of men." God takes away "an earthly crown and kingdom," yet "the gifts and calling" that are "of special grace are without repentance." Matthew Henry frames the same distinction in his memorable phrase: "Repentance in God is not, as it is in us, a change of his mind, but a change of his method... He does not alter his will, but wills an alteration. The change was in Saul: he has turned back from following me."

The grammatical-historical and wesleyan-arminian voices agree. Keil and Delitzsch say the words express "not any changeableness in the divine nature, but simply the sorrow of the divine love at the rebellion of sinners," as 1 Samuel 15:29 makes clear. Adam Clarke: "I intended, had he been obedient, to have established his kingdom. He has been disobedient; I change my purpose" — God's repenting is "changing a purpose according to conditions already laid down." Charles Ellicott gathers a chorus of the fathers and Reformers: Augustine, that "God does not feel the pain of remorse, nor is He ever deceived," but "as a man desires to make a change when he repents, so when God is said... to repent, we may expect a change from Him"; and Calvin, who explains Samuel's grief as fear for "the dishonour which would be inflicted upon the name of God," since Saul "had been chosen by God Himself," and his deposition threatened to unsettle the worship of Israel.

The turn all the witnesses take is toward Samuel's response, which they find as instructive as the oracle. He "cried unto the Lord all night." John Wesley reads it as intercession "to implore his pardoning mercy for Saul, and for the people," and finds in the phrase "is turned back" a proof that Saul "did once follow God." Henry paints the prophet on his knees while others slept, deprecating the sentence against his own successor, "so far was he from desiring that woeful day." From this the witnesses draw their pastoral point in common: "the rejection of sinners is the grief of good people; God delights not in their death, nor should we."
12:
The morning after the night of revelation, Samuel goes to find Saul, and the witnesses seize on the little monument the king has raised. John Gill pictures the prophet rising early, sleepless "since the revelation of the will of God was made unto him," and "in haste to converse with Saul." What meets him is the news that Saul, before coming down to Gilgal, has been to Carmel and there "set him up a place." Adam Clarke and Charles Ellicott both fasten on the striking Hebrew word: it is "literally, a hand" (yad) — a monument or triumphal marker — and they compare "Absalom's pillar," which 2 Samuel 18:18 likewise calls "the hand of Absalom." Ellicott, following the topography, places Carmel at Kurmul in Judah, south-east of Hebron, and suggests these "victory cairns or columns... had a hand engraved upon them." John Wesley simply glosses it "a monument or trophy of his victory."

What the witnesses hear in this detail is self-glory where there ought to have been self-examination. Matthew Henry is severe: Saul was "seeking his own honour more than the honour of God, for he set up this place for himself." The timing damns it — "he had more need to have been repenting of his sin and making his peace with God than boasting of his victory." Henry even hears pomp in the very verbs of the verse, the king marching "with a great deal of pomp and parade," gone about and passed on and gone down to Gilgal in state.

The contrast the commentators draw is stark and deliberate. At Gilgal — the place, Henry recalls, where Saul had been made king, and where he might now have been confirmed had he passed the trial — Samuel comes not to congratulate but to depose. The king erects a hand to point to his own triumph; the prophet arrives bearing the sentence of a triumph unfinished and a kingdom forfeited. The monument at Carmel becomes, in the witnesses' reading, an unwitting emblem of the whole man: busy commemorating a victory he had not fully won, and blind to the judgment already pronounced upon him in the night. Its raised "hand" points, they suggest, not to Saul's glory but to his fall.
13:
Saul's greeting to Samuel — "Blessed be thou of the Lord: I have performed the commandment of the Lord" — strikes the witnesses as a study in self-deceiving hypocrisy. John Gill sets the meeting at Gilgal. Keil and Delitzsch read the welcome as a screen: Saul "attempted to hide his consciousness of guilt by a feigned friendly welcome." The salutation itself is pious and traditional — "Blessed be thou of the Lord" echoes the blessings of Ruth 2:20 and Genesis 14:19 — but it is spread over a bad conscience.

Charles Ellicott exposes the inner contradiction with care. Saul "must have been fully conscious that he had failed to carry out the will and command of the Eternal King of Israel." In a war "undertaken for the definite and solemn purpose of exterminating a wicked and bloodthirsty people," he had "disregarded the express instructions of the prophet... for his own covetous purposes," reserving "some of the living spoil for himself." And yet, "conscious of all this, he still dared to come forward, and to congratulate the prophet upon the fulfilment of the Lord's command." The claim is a lie the speaker half-believes: "in his heart he knew he had been faithless."

The witnesses find in this the perennial posture of the unhumbled religious man. Matthew Henry, weighing Saul's boast, observes that "it is very likely, if his conscience had now flown in his face... he would not have been so forward to proclaim his obedience"; rather, "by this he hoped to prevent Samuel's reproving him." So, Henry adds, "sinners think, by justifying themselves, to escape being judged of the Lord, whereas the only way to do that is by judging ourselves." The verse becomes a mirror: the loud profession of having "performed the commandment" is precisely the mark of one who has not, and who dreads the reproof he rushes to forestall. Saul's confidence is not innocence but its counterfeit — a man announcing his obedience the more insistently because he knows it has failed. The very fervour of "I have performed the commandment" invites the question that the next verse will pose, and that Saul cannot answer.
14:
Samuel's reply needs no argument, only an ear: "What meaneth then this bleating of the sheep in mine ears, and the lowing of the oxen which I hear?" The witnesses delight in the irony. John Gill puts the logic simply: "the orders were to destroy all living creatures belonging to the Amalekites," so "if therefore Saul had performed the commandment of the Lord, as he said he had, from whence were these sheep Samuel heard bleating?" Keil and Delitzsch say "the prophet stripped his hypocrisy at once," for "the falsehood and hypocrisy of these words lay upon the very surface."

Charles Ellicott, quoting Lange, catches the tone exactly: "Saul is convicted of falsehood by the voices of the animals which he has spared, contrary to God's command," and Samuel's manner of "citing them against him... has an air of holy humour and cutting irony." The spared cattle, meant to be the material of a splendid sacrifice, become instead the witnesses for the prosecution — creation itself testifying against the man who claimed to have obeyed.

Matthew Henry develops the point into a warning for every hypocrite. The bleating and lowing, brought up perhaps "to bring up the rear of his triumph," Samuel "appears to as witnesses against him." "The noise the cattle made (like the rust of silver, James 5:3) would be a witness against him." And Henry presses the application home: "it is no new thing for the plausible professions and protestations of hypocrites to be contradicted and disproved by the most plain and undeniable evidence. Many boast of their obedience to the command of God; but what mean then their indulgence of the flesh, their love of the world, their passion and uncharitableness, and their neglect of holy duties, which witness against them?" The witnesses thus turn a single question into a principle of judgment: profession may be loud, but conduct speaks, and the plain facts of a life will drown out its protestations. Saul had scarcely finished claiming full obedience before the very spoils he kept began, with their bleating, to give him the lie.
15:
Caught, Saul reaches at once for two defenses — it was the people's doing, and it was for God — and the witnesses find both hollow. John Gill notices how quickly the king shifts the guilt: "laying the blame on them, as Adam did on his wife, as if he had no concern at all in it," though "it is not probable the people should do this... without his consent and authority." The second plea, that the cattle were kept "to sacrifice unto the Lord thy God," Gill treats as a hasty attempt "to excuse the people as well as he could."

Keil and Delitzsch take apart the religious pretext. Even granting the animals were meant for sacrifice, "not only the people, but Saul also, would have had their own interests in view," since "the flesh of thank-offerings was appropriated to sacrificial meals" — the piety would have fed the worshippers. Adam Clarke concedes the people "did spare the best of the prey," but insists Saul "might have restrained them if he would," and recalls that God had "interdicted spoil and plunder" precisely so the war would be waged "merely from a sense of duty, without any hope of enriching themselves by it."

The sharpest reading is Charles Ellicott's, who hears in Saul's answer the cowardice of a divided heart. The king, understanding at once "the drift of his old friend's words," replies in effect, "this reservation, which you condemn, was insisted upon by the people; and their object... was to do special honour to God in a great sacrifice." Ellicott judges it plainly: "There seems something strangely cowardly in this trying to transfer from himself to the people the blame of disobedience to the Divine commands. It is unlike Saul's old character; but covetousness and vanity invariably lead to moral cowardice." The witnesses note the telling little word in Saul's mouth — "the Lord thy God," not my God — the distancing of a man who wraps his self-interest in a religious excuse while quietly holding the Lord at arm's length. Blame-shifting and false devotion, they conclude, are the twin refuges of the impenitent, and Saul has fled to both at once.
16:
Samuel cuts through the excuses with a single command — "Stay, and I will tell thee what the Lord hath said to me this night" — and the witnesses mark the shift of authority in the room. John Gill hears in "stay" a double note: "stop a little, do not be in haste to be gone," and also "suffer me," that is, "give me leave to say a few words." Gill observes the courtesy in it — "Saul being a king, Samuel treats him as such, and asks audience of him," seeking "leave to deliver what he had to say." Keil and Delitzsch render the Hebrew harpe more curtly, "leave off, excusing thyself any further," and note that Samuel is about to recall Saul's own humble words, "when thou wast little in thine eyes" (1 Samuel 9:21).

Charles Ellicott catches the drama of the moment. The king "was probably turning away, desirous of closing an interview which to him was full of bitterness, when he was arrested by the solemn words, and probably by the commanding gesture, of his old friend and counsellor, who now addressed him with the majesty and power of an accredited servant of the Most High." The private friend has become the public ambassador; the counsellor speaks now as the mouth of God.

The phrase the witnesses linger over is "this night." It reaches back to the secret revelation of 1 Samuel 15:10 and to Samuel's own night of intercession in 1 Samuel 15:11. What Saul is about to hear is not Samuel's private displeasure but the verdict God gave the prophet while the king slept boasting at Carmel. Saul's brief answer, "Say on," is, in the commentators' reading, the last courtesy of a doomed man — the king granting audience to the very word that will unmake him. The exchange marks the moment the initiative passes wholly out of Saul's hands: he had been managing the interview with greetings and excuses; now he can only listen. Samuel demands silence, and gets it, and into that silence he will speak the sentence of heaven.
17:
Samuel begins the indictment where Saul's greatness began — in his own former smallness. "When thou wast little in thine own sight, wast thou not made the head of the tribes of Israel?" John Gill reads the reminder as a rebuke by contrast: once Saul was "humble and lowly, and had a mean opinion of himself, his family and tribe, and judged himself unworthy of the kingdom"; the words suggest "that now he was proud and haughty, and would have his own will and way." Keil and Delitzsch lay out the sequence Samuel rehearses — made "the head of the tribes," anointed king, and "sent on a journey" with the plain charge to destroy "the sinners the Amalekites."

Adam Clarke turns the verse into a maxim on prosperity: "Little in thine own sight — Who can bear prosperity?" The very success that should have deepened Saul's dependence has instead puffed him up. Charles Ellicott weighs, and rejects, an alternative reading. Kimchi construed the Hebrew to mean, "Though thou seemest to thyself too little and weak to curb the people, yet wast thou the head, and shouldest have done thy duty" — but Ellicott follows Lange in objecting that this "would imply that Samuel had accepted Saul's excuse that it was the people's will to reserve the choicest spoil." The prophet grants no such thing.

On the plain sense, the witnesses are agreed. Samuel's words, Ellicott says, "were simply to remind Saul that the Lord, whose clearly expressed will he had disregarded, had raised him in bygone days from a comparatively humble station to the proud position he was then occupying as chief of Israel." There had been "a time when he judged himself unequal to this great work"; "but now, how strange the contrast! Flushed with success, he was trusting alone in his unaided strength, and openly disobeying the Divine commands." The verse thus diagnoses the root beneath the day's disobedience. It was pride — the swelling of a man who forgot that his crown was a gift and began to treat it as a possession. Samuel reasons from Saul's own history: the God who had exalted the lowly Benjamite had every right to be obeyed by the king he had made, and the forgetting of that first smallness is the beginning of the fall.
18:
Samuel now restates the commission Saul had been given, so that the disobedience may be measured against the plain terms of the charge. "The Lord sent thee on a journey, and said, Go and utterly destroy the sinners the Amalekites." John Gill draws the obvious inference from the word "sent": Saul was dispatched on an errand, "and therefore he ought to have attended to the errand sent upon, and executed the orders given"; it was "in vain, therefore, to lay the blame on the people." The responsibility lay with the one commissioned, not with those he led.

Charles Ellicott fastens on the description of the enemy. This verse "briefly rehearses the charge of the Most High, which Saul had deliberately disobeyed," and he finds it "noticeable that the Amalekites are expressly called 'sinners,' thus indicating the reason of the Divine wrath against them." The word is not incidental: as "the men of Sodom" were "styled 'sinners before the Lord'" in Genesis 13:13, so Amalek is named by the very quality that has ripened its judgment. The command to destroy is not caprice but sentence; the people marked for the ban are marked as sinners whose iniquity is full.

Matthew Henry underlines how light the charge really was, and how inexcusable the failure. "The Lord sent thee on a journey," he notes — "so easy was the service, and so certain the success, that it was rather to be called a journey than a war." And the work itself was honourable, "to destroy the sworn enemies of God and Israel." Had Saul only denied himself and destroyed all that belonged to Amalek, "he would have been no loser by it at last," for "God would no doubt have made it up to him." The witnesses thus strip away every mitigation. The task was plain, the enemy justly doomed, the outcome assured, and the servant personally charged; against so clear a commission, Saul's talk of the people's will collapses. What God had defined in a sentence, Saul had every means to obey, and every reason to. The measure of his sin is the simplicity of the command he broke.
19:
The prophet's question drives to the heart of the matter: "Wherefore then didst thou not obey the voice of the Lord, but didst fly upon the spoil, and didst evil in the sight of the Lord?" John Gill heightens the ingratitude by naming who was disobeyed — the Lord "who had made him king, and sent him on this errand, and gave him such plain directions, and such strong orders to make an entire consumption of Amalek." The clearer the charge, the darker the refusal.

Charles Ellicott weighs the vivid verb "didst fly upon the spoil." The expression, he says, "evidently includes the idea of greedy eagerness, as though Samuel detected a spirit of grasping covetousness at the bottom of this disobedient act of Saul's." Beneath the pious talk of sacrifice, the prophet sees the true motive — a hungry pouncing on the plunder that God had devoted to destruction.

Matthew Henry presses the diagnosis furthest, and it is here that the witnesses locate the essence of the sin. "Wherefore then didst thou fly upon the spoil, and convert that to thy own use which was to have been destroyed for God's honour? See what evil the love of money is the root of." But Henry does not let covetousness be the last word; he digs to the deeper offense: "See what is the sinfulness of sin, and that in it which above any thing else makes it evil in the sight of the Lord. It is disobedience: Thou didst not obey the voice of the Lord." That, for the commentators, is the nerve of the whole chapter. Saul's covetousness and vanity were real, but the guilt that damns the act is not chiefly its greed; it is that a plain command of God was set aside. The spoil was only the occasion; the rebellion was the crime. Samuel's threefold charge — did not obey, flew upon the spoil, did evil in the sight of the Lord — moves from the outward act inward, until it rests where all sin finally rests, in a will that would not bend to the voice of God.
20:
Instead of confessing, Saul doubles down, and the witnesses read his interruption as the reflex of a self-deceived heart. John Gill notes that "Saul breaks in upon Samuel before he had declared all that the Lord had said unto him," and, "with the utmost assurance affirms he had obeyed the voice of the Lord" — though the proof he offers is "very imperfect," and "poor proof does he give of it." Keil and Delitzsch parse the emphatic Hebrew (asher serving here "in the sense of asseveration"): "Yea, I have hearkened to the voice of Jehovah... and have brought Agag the king of the Amalekites, and banned Amalek." Bringing back Agag, they observe, "he mentioned probably as a practical proof that he had carried out the war of extermination" — as if the captive king were evidence of obedience rather than the very sign of its breach.

Charles Ellicott exposes the state of mind beneath the claim. These words "are simply a repetition of the king's former excuse, but they show us what was the state of Saul's mind: he evidently disbelieved in the power of the Eternal as a heart reader." Saul cared only to "justify himself before Samuel" — "that was all he cared for." So "he asserted his own integrity of purpose and his great zeal for the public sacrifice to God, knowing all the while that low earthly reasons had been the springs of his conduct," and "reiterated the plea that what he had done was in accordance with the voice of the people, conscious all the while that the plea was false."

What the witnesses find most chilling is the boldness of the self-justification. Saul does not merely minimize his fault; he claims, flatly, "I have obeyed the voice of the Lord," and produces the spared king as though it vindicated him. The commentators see a man who has argued himself into believing his own defense, or who at least hopes that the prophet — and perhaps God — can be managed by a confident face and a plausible story. It is the posture Henry named at the greeting: sinners "think, by justifying themselves, to escape being judged of the Lord." Saul's tragedy deepens here, for he is offered, in Samuel's pause, the very opening for repentance, and fills it instead with a fresh assertion of his innocence.
21:
Saul's final excuse retreats once more behind the people and the altar: "But the people took of the spoil, sheep and oxen... to sacrifice unto the Lord thy God in Gilgal." John Gill repeats the charge against the king's evasion — "still he continues to lay the blame on the people, when he, as king, ought to have restrained them." John Wesley detects a first, faint stirring of conscience even so: "Here the conscience of Saul begins to awake, though but a little; for he still lays the blame on the people." The awakening is real but partial, still tangled in self-defense.

Adam Clarke lets the pretext state itself in its most plausible form: "To sacrifice unto the Lord — thus he endeavours to excuse the people. They did not take the spoil in order to enrich themselves by it, but to sacrifice unto the Lord; and did not this motive justify their conduct?" The question is Saul's, and the witnesses answer it with one voice: no.

Keil and Delitzsch give the decisive refutation, and it turns on the very nature of the ban. Saul "endeavoured to defend" the sparing "as the fulfilment of a religious duty," the people having taken the best "as firstlings of the ban, to sacrifice to Jehovah." Now "sacrificing the best of the booty taken in war as an offering of first-fruits to the Lord... was a praiseworthy sign of piety" in other circumstances (compare Numbers 31:48). "Only he overlooked the fact that what was banned to the Lord could not be offered to Him as a burnt-offering, because, being most holy, it belonged to Him already" (Leviticus 27:29), "and, according to Deuteronomy 13:16, was to be put to death, as Samuel had expressly said to Saul" in 1 Samuel 15:3. The pretext collapses on its own logic. A thing devoted to destruction cannot be re-devoted as a gift; to offer the banned cattle in sacrifice was not to honour God but to rob him under a religious name. The witnesses thus close every door: the spoil was not the people's to keep, not Saul's to allow, and not fit to lay on any altar. What looked like zeal was disobedience dressed as worship.
22:
Samuel answers the whole chain of excuses with the great oracle of the chapter: "Hath the Lord as great delight in burnt offerings and sacrifices, as in obeying the voice of the Lord? Behold, to obey is better than sacrifice, and to hearken than the fat of rams." The witnesses treat this as the theological summit of the narrative. Keil and Delitzsch guard it from misuse: Samuel "did not reject sacrifices as worthless," nor say "that God took no pleasure in burnt-offerings," but "simply compared sacrifice with obedience... and pronounced the latter of greater worth," so that "the sum and substance of divine worship consisted in obedience." Adam Clarke calls it "a very proper answer to, and refutation of Saul's excuse": "Is not obedience to the will of God the end of all religion, of its rites, ceremonies, and sacrifices?"

Matthew Henry unfolds the contrast at length. Obedience is enjoined "by the eternal law of nature," while "sacrifice supposes sin come into the world, and is but a feeble attempt to take that away which obedience would have prevented." "A careful conformity to moral precepts recommends us to God more than all ceremonial observances" (Micah 6:6–8; Hosea 6:6); "it is much easier to bring a bullock or lamb to be burnt upon the altar than to bring every high thought into obedience to God, and the will subject to his will." Obedience, he adds, "is the glory of angels" (Psalms 103:20), and it will be ours.

Charles Ellicott hears the Spirit descending on the prophet in "one of those rapt expressions" that echo through the whole prophetic tradition — reproduced or referred to in Psalms 50:8–14, Isaiah 1:11, Jeremiah 6:20, and confirmed by our Lord himself in Matthew 9:13. He quotes Irenaeus, that the saying foretold the day when "the burnt offerings enjoined on Israel would give place to a simple worship of the heart," and Gregory's weighty comment: "In sacrifices a man offers only strange flesh, whereas in obedience he offers his own will." John Wesley completes the thought: obedience "is a moral duty, constantly and indispensably necessary," while sacrifice "is but a ceremonial institution, sometimes unnecessary... and sometimes sinful, when it is offered by a polluted hand." The witnesses stand united on the principle that judges Saul and every worshipper after him: God will not be bought off with gifts by a will that refuses to bend to his voice.
23:
Samuel presses the oracle to its terrible conclusion: "Rebellion is as the sin of witchcraft, and stubbornness is as iniquity and idolatry. Because thou hast rejected the word of the Lord, he hath also rejected thee from being king." John Gill weighs the comparison: rebellion against God, "though in things otherwise lawful," is "as heinous a sin as to be guilty of witchcraft, or any kind of divination forbidden by the law of God, and deserves as sore a punishment." John Wesley defines the terms — rebellion is "disobedience to God's command," and stubbornness "contumacy in sin, justifying it, and pleading for it" — and notes that the rejection is a sentence pronounced, not yet an actual deposition, "for that he was not actually deposed by God before plainly appears, because not only the people, but even David, after this, owned him as king."

Charles Ellicott presses the equation to its root. Witchcraft, "more literally soothsaying or divination," was "the greatest of all the dangers to which Israel was exposed... a tampering with the idol-worship of the surrounding nations." Hence "rebellion, or conscious disobedience to the express commands of the Eternal, in the case of Saul... was nothing else than the deadly sin of idol-worship, for it set aside the true Master of Israel, and virtually acknowledged another." He explains the Hebrew: aven, rendered "iniquity," "literally signifies nothingness" and is later used for an idol (Hosea 10:8), and teraphim were "small household gods or idols," like the Roman Lares.

One voice stands somewhat apart in method. Adam Clarke, finding the Authorised Version "no translation of those difficult words," proposes that "the three nouns which occur first in the text refer each to the three last in order" — transgression to iniquity, divination to teraphim, rebellion to stubbornness — reading, "as transgression comes from iniquity, divination from teraphim, and rebellion from stubbornness, so, because thou hast rejected the word of the Lord, he hath also rejected thee." Matthew Henry gathers the theological point the others share: those "governed by their own corrupt inclinations, in opposition to the command of God, do, in effect, consult the teraphim"; and "it was disobedience that made us all sinners" (Romans 5:19), which is why sin is "enmity to God" (Romans 8:7). The sentence lands where the chapter has been driving: to set one's own will against God's is to un-god him and to enthrone a rival, and the king who will not be ruled is unfit to rule.
24:
At last Saul says the words "I have sinned," and here the witnesses openly divide over whether the confession is real. Matthew Henry is convinced it is only "the dress of the penitent." He counts "several bad signs of the hypocrisy of his repentance." First, Saul "made his application to Samuel only," confessing that he "transgressed the commandment of the Lord and thy word" — "as if he had been in God's stead" — whereas David, convicted by Nathan, cried, "Against thee only have I sinned" (Psalms 51:4). Second, "he excused his fault even in the confession of it," pleading "I feared the people," which "is never the fashion of a true penitent." Third, "all his care was to save his credit, and preserve his interest in the people." John Gill agrees the confession is "not ingenuous, cordial, and sincere," but "made chiefly for the sake of getting the sentence... reversed."

Charles Ellicott stands with the darker reading while probing the psychology. "The grave condemnation of the prophet appalled the king," and the thought that "in the eyes of the Invisible and Eternal, he ranked with the idolators and heathen sinners around, was... terrible." Yet even now, "with stammering lips, while deprecating the Divine sentence, he still seeks to justify himself"; all he can finally plead is "that he loved the praise of men more than the approval of his God."

Against this weight of suspicion stands John Wesley, who defends Saul's sincerity. "It does by no means appear," he writes, "that Saul acts the hypocrite herein, in assigning a false cause of his disobedience. Rather, he nakedly declares the thing as it was." Where Henry and Gill hear a self-serving excuse in "I feared the people, and obeyed their voice," Wesley hears an honest, if humbling, account of what actually moved him. The debate is a genuine one, and the witnesses let it stand: was Saul's "I have sinned" the first crack of a broken heart, or only the calculation of a cornered king? Most read it as the latter — a confession that keeps one eye on the throne — while Wesley, in charity, takes it at face value. What none disputes is that even this confession is entangled with excuse, and that the sentence Samuel has pronounced is not thereby recalled.
25:
Saul follows his confession with a plea: "Now therefore, I pray thee, pardon my sin, and turn again with me, that I may worship the Lord." The witnesses ask what Saul can have meant by asking a man to "pardon" his sin. John Gill reasons that Saul could hardly imagine Samuel able to forgive sin "as committed against God, which none but God can do"; he meant either that Samuel should forgive "so far as he had offended him," or, "as a prophet of the Lord," should "make use of his interest" and "pray to God that his sin might be forgiven, and the sentence reversed." Indeed, Gill suspects, "the pardon of his sin" chiefly means "the averting of a threatened judgment" — the reversal of his rejection. Adam Clarke reads the Hebrew as "bear my sin; take it away," and hears Saul asking the prophet to "be my intercessor with God."

Charles Ellicott lays bare the true spring of the request: "the sorrow of Saul was rather for the immediate earthly consequence which he feared might follow the Divine rejection. He foresaw his power in Israel would sensibly decrease," and so "he intreats the great prophet not to desert him." Keil and Delitzsch record the outcome — "this request Samuel refused, repeating at the same time the sentence of rejection, and turned to depart."

Yet the reading is not unanimous, for John Wesley again pleads for Saul's good faith. "Neither can it be proved," he writes, "that there was any hypocrisy in this. Rather charity requires us to believe that he sincerely desired pardon, both from God and man, as he now knew he had sinned against both." The witnesses thus keep the tension of the previous verse alive. To most of them Saul's prayer is transparent: it seeks the prophet's company chiefly to shore up the king's standing, and "pardon" means little more than the lifting of a penalty. To Wesley it is the cry of a man who, however weak, truly wants to be forgiven. Between the two the verse hangs, a request for mercy that may be sincere and may be self-interested — and that Samuel, reading the heart, declines.
26:
Samuel's refusal is flat: "I will not return with thee: for thou hast rejected the word of the Lord, and the Lord hath rejected thee from being king over Israel." John Gill explains why the prophet turns away — he was "not satisfied with his repentance and confession," Saul "still extenuating his sin, and laying the blame of it on the people." Gill takes the refusal as spoken "by way of resentment, and as expressing his indignation," and immediately guards a difficulty: Samuel "afterwards did return with him on a change of his mind, which a good man may be allowed to make, without any imputation of falsehood or a lie unto him." Adam Clarke puts the reason of the refusal on public grounds: "I cannot acknowledge thee as king, seeing the Lord hath rejected thee."

Charles Ellicott reads the prophet's discernment: "Samuel too clearly sees what are the true springs of Saul's repentance, and refuses at first." Following C. a Lapide, he finds those springs to be "a fear on the part of the king of losing the kingdom and of incurring public disgrace," and so "the prophet for reply again repeats the terrible Divine sentence of rejection."

John Wesley takes up the very point Gill raised, and turns Samuel's "I will not return" into a small case of conscience. "This was no lie," he insists, "though he afterwards returned, because he spoke what he meant; his words and his intentions agreed together, though afterwards he saw reason to change his intentions" (compare Genesis 19:2–3). And he draws a pastoral comfort from it: "this may relieve many perplexed consciences, who think themselves obliged to do what they have said they would do, though they see just cause to change their minds." The witnesses thus hold two things together. Samuel's word is a true refusal, honestly meant in the moment, and yet not a binding oath that forbids a later, better change. Meanwhile Wesley marks the limit of the sentence Samuel repeats: the Lord "hath rejected thee" from the throne — "but he does not say, he hath rejected thee from salvation. And who besides hath authority to say so?" The kingdom is forfeit; the man's eternal state is left in God's hands.
27:
As Samuel turns to go, Saul clutches at him and tears his robe, and the witnesses read the accident as the pivot of the scene. John Gill pictures the prophet turning "a different way from Gilgal, perhaps towards his own city Ramah, with an intention to have nothing more to say to Saul... so displeased was he with him." Keil and Delitzsch settle a textual point: Saul "laid hold of the lappet of his mantle, and it tore" — the passive "was torn off" is correct, as Samuel's own explanation in the next verse proves; this was no deliberate rending by Saul but a garment that gave way in his grasp.

Charles Ellicott reads the desperation in the gesture: "the king's passionate action indicates a restless, unquiet mind. Not content with intreating words, Saul, perhaps even with some violence, lays hold of the old man as he turns away, to detain him." He adds a careful note on the garment, following the Dean of Canterbury: what Saul seized was "not the mantle... but the hem, or outer border, of the meil," the ordinary tunic of the upper classes. The distinctive prophetic dress of coarse camel's-hair "came into use only gradually," Elijah being "the first person described as being thus clad," and the line running down at last "to Israel's last prophet, John the Baptist" (Mark 1:6).

Matthew Henry catches the pathos and the providence together. Saul "tore his clothes to detain him... so loth was he to part with the prophet" — not, Henry suspects, from love of Samuel himself, but because "he feared that if Samuel forsook him the people would do so too." And then the turn: Samuel "put a construction upon this accident which none but a prophet could." A snatched hem, a torn seam — the sort of small mishap that means nothing — becomes in the prophet's mouth the sign of the kingdom torn away. The witnesses savor the irony: Saul, grasping to hold Samuel and his own tottering authority, with his own hand supplies the emblem of his loss. What he meant as a clutch of desperate retention becomes the visible token of divine rejection, which Samuel will now interpret.
28:
Samuel seizes on the torn hem and makes it prophecy: "The Lord hath rent the kingdom of Israel from thee this day, and hath given it to a neighbour of thine, that is better than thou." John Gill explains the move — Samuel "took occasion" from the rent garment "to predict, and no doubt it was impressed on his mind by the Spirit of God, that his kingdom should be in a like manner rent from him." The sentence is settled: "from this day forward he might expect it; the sentence was gone forth from God, and it would not be reversed." Gill compares the like sign given later, when the tearing of a garment foretold the rending of ten tribes from the house of David (1 Kings 11:30–31).

Charles Ellicott reads the same symbolism: "the prophet at once looks upon the garment torn by the passionate vehemence of the king as an omen for the future, and uses the rent vesture as a symbol, to show Saul that thus had the Lord on that day rent the kingdom from him." He notes the studied vagueness of the recipient — "it had not yet been revealed to the seer who was to replace the rebellious king, so he simply refers to the future anointed one quite indefinitely as 'a neighbour.'"

Matthew Henry fills in the name the prophecy withholds and traces its uncanny echo. The rending "was his own doing," Henry says: "he hath rent it from thee, and given it to a neighbour better than thou" — "namely, to David, who afterwards, upon occasion, cut off the skirt of Saul's robe" (1 Samuel 24:4), "upon which Saul said, I know that thou shalt surely be king" (1 Samuel 24:20), "perhaps remembering this sign, the tearing of the skirt of Samuel's mantle." The witnesses relish the symmetry the narrative sets up. Here a torn robe declares the kingdom lost; years later, a torn robe in a cave will declare it found, and Saul himself will read the omen aright. The verse fixes the point of no return: not merely a threat now, but a divine act announced — the crown taken and already assigned, though the "neighbour" is left, for the moment, unnamed.
29:
Samuel seals the sentence with a title for God: "And also the Strength of Israel will not lie nor repent: for he is not a man, that he should repent." The witnesses divide, gently, over how to render that title, and unite in reconciling it with the "repenting" of 1 Samuel 15:11. John Gill notes the many possibilities — "the Strength of Israel," or "the victory of Israel," or "the eternity of Israel" — and reasons from the name: God "cannot lie, which is the effect of weakness; nor repent or change his mind, as men do, when something unforeseen arises." Matthew Henry collects the same range: "the Eternity or Victory of Israel, so some read it; the holy One, so the Arabic; the most noble One, so the Syriac; the triumphant King of Israel, so bishop Patrick." His point is the contrast with human frailty: "men are fickle and alter their minds, feeble and cannot effect their purposes... but with God it is not so."

Charles Ellicott prefers "the Changeless One of Israel," noting the Hebrew word "is first found in this passage" and is elsewhere rendered "glory" (1 Chronicles 29:2). He meets the apparent contradiction squarely: in 1 Samuel 15:11 "we read of the Eternal saying, It repenteth me... while here we find how the Changeless One of Israel will not repent." The resolution is that "with God there is no change... the change springs alone from a change in the men themselves, not in God." As Dean Payne Smith puts it, "one instrument may be laid aside, and another chosen (as was the case of Saul), because God ordains that the instruments by which He works shall be beings endowed with free will." Predictions and promises "are never absolute, but are always conditional"; still, "I am Jehovah; I change not" (Malachi 3:6), and "the counsel of the Lord stands for ever" (Psalms 33:11).

John Wesley grounds the whole in God's self-sufficiency: God cannot lie, "because lying proceeds from the sense of a man's weakness, who cannot many times accomplish his design without lying"; but God "needs no such artifices; he can do whatsoever he pleaseth by his absolute power." Adam Clarke adds that "what God has purposed he will bring to pass," and slips in a word of realism about Saul — "who could resist the demands of a victorious mob? But his crime was in consenting." The witnesses thus turn a divine name into a doctrine: the God who seems to "repent" of Saul is precisely the God who never wavers, whose apparent change is only the constancy of his holiness meeting the real change in a rebellious man.
30:
Saul confesses a second time, but the words that follow give him away: "I have sinned: yet honour me now, I pray thee, before the elders of my people, and before Israel." John Gill marks the difference from the earlier confession: here Saul says "I have sinned" without the extenuation he had added before, "and yet by what follows it appears to be not ingenuous and sincere, but hypocritical." The plea for public honour betrays what the bare confession seemed to hide.

Charles Ellicott reads the request as the key to Saul's whole penitence: "it was a strange penitence, after all, this sorrow of Saul for his great sin. He was, no doubt, terribly in earnest and in great fear; but his earnestness was based upon a desire to maintain his power and royal state, and his fear sprang from a well-grounded apprehension that if he lost the countenance of Samuel the seer... he would probably forfeit his crown." Ellicott clinches it with Gregory's judgment: "If Saul had been really penitent, he would pray to have been humble rather than to be honoured."

Matthew Henry presses the contradiction hardest, and in doing so states the anatomy of true and false repentance. Saul's word, he says, "was very gross": "'I have sinned, yet honour me, I pray thee, before my people.' Is this the language of a penitent? No, but the contrary." The genuine penitent says rather, "I have sinned, shame me now, for to me belongs shame, and no man can loathe me so much as I loathe myself." Yet, Henry laments, "how often do we meet with the copies of this hypocrisy of Saul! It is very common for those who are convicted of sin to show themselves very solicitous to be honoured before the people." He draws the rule the witnesses share: a man who has lost the honour of the innocent "can pretend to no other than that of a penitent, and it is the honour of a penitent to take shame to himself." Saul's tragedy, in their reading, is that even his contrition is bent toward the crowd. He grieves for his standing more than for his sin, and asks to be honoured in the very breath that owns his guilt — proof that the sorrow has not reached the heart.
31:
Samuel yields to Saul's entreaty and turns back, and the witnesses ask why — since he had so firmly refused — he now consents. John Gill secures the honesty of the reversal: Samuel "turned again though he before said he would not, yet he did, changing his mind, as he might without being chargeable with a lie." His purpose, Gill thinks, was not to worship alongside Saul — "it does not appear that he did, but rather the contrary" — but "that Saul might not be despised by the people, and his authority lessened, while he continued king; and that he might do what Saul had neglected to do, destroy Agag." John Wesley gives the same twin reasons: "that the people might not, upon pretence of this sentence of rejection, withdraw their obedience to their sovereign," and "that he might rectify Saul's error, and execute God's judgment upon Agag."

Keil and Delitzsch agree that Samuel "remained, not merely for the purpose of preserving the outward order until a new king should take his place... but also to carry out the ban upon Agag, whom Saul had spared." Matthew Henry adds his careful qualification: Samuel turned again "upon further thoughts, and probably by divine direction, either to prevent a mutiny among the people" or "to do justice on Agag"; and though "Saul worshipped the Lord, it is not said Samuel presided in that worship."

Charles Ellicott widens the political horizon. The prophet's "principal motive... was a desire to prevent any disaffection towards the monarchy," for "his known disapproval of Saul's conduct, and his declining the king's earnest prayer to stay, would probably have been the signal to the discontented spirits in Israel to revolt," which "in those critical times would have been disastrous." So Saul "remained in undiminished power apparently; but the will of God, as declared by His servant Samuel, was slowly, but surely, accomplished." The witnesses thus read Samuel's return not as a softening of the sentence but as a wise stewardship of it. The doom stands; the prophet simply keeps the peace of the kingdom and stays to finish the work of judgment that the king had left undone. Saul bows in worship, but the initiative has passed to the prophet, who has one act of obedience still to perform in Saul's place.
32:
The scene closes with the doomed king of Amalek led out, and the witnesses read Agag's bearing and his famous last words with care. "Agag came unto him delicately. And Agag said, Surely the bitterness of death is past." Matthew Henry hears both a king's dignity and a fool's false hope: Agag "came delicately, in a stately manner, to show that he was a king," or "in a soft effeminate manner, as one never used to hardship," hoping to "move compassion." He supposed that, "having escaped the sword of Saul, that man of war, he was in no danger from Samuel, an old prophet, a man of peace." Henry turns it to a warning: "many think the bitterness of death is past when it is not so; they put that evil day far from them which is very near."

The grammatical-historical voices weigh the difficult words. Adam Clarke reports that "the Septuagint have trembling," while the Hebrew maadannoth means "delicacies" — "a man of delights, a pleasure-taker" — and the Vulgate reads "very fat and trembling"; and he judges that "almost all the versions" render the last clause not as confidence but as lament: "Surely death is bitter." Charles Ellicott canvasses the same range: the word for "delicately" may come "from the same root as Eden, the garden of joy," giving "cheerfully, gladly," or from another root yielding "in bands or in fetters," while "the LXX. must have found another word altogether, for they render it trembling." John Gill simply sets the command in its setting — Samuel spoke it "to some of Saul's officers... before all the people met together for sacrifice."

Ellicott and Henry also probe why Saul had spared him. Ellicott offers Ewald's shrewd guess that "kings, for the honour of their craft, must spare each other," and observes that from Samuel's words in the next verse Agag "seems, even among a wicked race, to have been pre-eminent in wickedness." Whether Agag came strutting in relief or shuffling in dread, the witnesses agree on the pathos of the moment: the one life Saul's disobedience had preserved is the very life that must now be taken, and the king who thought his death was behind him finds it standing before him in the person of the old prophet.
33:
Samuel pronounces the sentence and executes it: "As thy sword hath made women childless, so shall thy mother be childless among women. And Samuel hewed Agag in pieces before the Lord in Gilgal." The witnesses insist the death is just retribution for Agag's own crimes, not merely his nation's. John Gill reads the sentence as proof "that he was a cruel prince, and justly died for his own barbarity and wickedness, as well as for the sins of his ancestors four hundred years ago." Keil and Delitzsch take the Hebrew as a comparative — "more childless than other women, i.e., the most childless of women, namely, because her son was the king" — and conclude that "Agag had carried on his wars with great cruelty, and had therefore forfeited his life according to the lex talionis." The execution was "before the Lord at Gilgal," that is, before his altar, "for the slaying of Agag, being the execution of the ban, was an act performed for the glory of God."

John Wesley makes the same point the vindication of divine justice: the sentence shows "that although God did at this time revenge a crime committed by this man's ancestors four hundred years ago, yet he did not punish an innocent son for his father's crimes, but one that persisted in the same evil courses." The generation's own guilt, not merely inherited guilt, is what the sword answers.

A quieter question divides the commentators: did Samuel himself wield the blade? Adam Clarke thinks not — "what Samuel did here he did in his magisterial capacity; and it is not likely he did it by his own sword, but by that of an executioner," on the maxim "qui facit per alterum, facit per se," he who acts through another acts himself. Charles Ellicott suggests the words "refer to a peculiar form of putting to death, like the quartering in vogue during the Middle Ages." Matthew Henry, taking the act more literally, treats it as done "by such special direction from heaven as none now can pretend to," a sacrifice "to God's injured justice." Wesley adds the necessary caution against misuse: Samuel acted "by divine instinct, and in pursuance of God's express command," so that "these are no precedents for private persons to take the sword of justice into their hands." The witnesses close the episode agreed that a real and righteous judgment fell, completing the obedience Saul had refused — the last stroke of the ban, delivered by the prophet because the king would not.
34:
The narrative parts the two men to their separate homes: "Then Samuel went to Ramah; and Saul went up to his house to Gibeah of Saul." John Gill notes only that Ramah was Samuel's "native place, and where was his usual residence." But the witnesses hear in the quiet geography the sound of a final separation. Keil and Delitzsch read the verse as the mark of a breach: "from that time forward Samuel broke off all intercourse with the king whom Jehovah had rejected."

The reason they give for that breach is a tender one. It was "because Samuel had loved Saul on account of his previous election"; "and yet, as Jehovah had rejected him unconditionally, he felt that he was precluded from doing anything to effect a change of heart in Saul, and his reinstatement as king." Samuel's withdrawal, in this reading, is not coldness but grief bound by the divine sentence. He may not undo what God has done, and so the prophet who anointed the king must now leave him. The verse looks forward to its own explanation in the words that immediately follow — "Samuel mourned for Saul, and the Lord repented that he had made Saul king" — so that the parting at Gibeah and Ramah is charged with sorrow on both sides of the road.

The witnesses let the two houses stand as emblems. Saul goes up to Gibeah still wearing a crown that has been forfeited in heaven, a king in fact whose kingship has lost its warrant. Samuel goes home to Ramah to mourn and to wait for the "neighbour" not yet named. Between the boastful monument at Carmel with which the day began and this silent homeward journey with which it ends, everything has changed: the campaign was won, the ban half-kept and then completed by the prophet's hand, the sentence pronounced, the robe torn, the last enemy slain. What remains is only the long, sad outworking of a rejection already sealed — and the verse, with its bare naming of two towns, quietly begins it. Keil and Delitzsch add that the parting was permanent in intent: Samuel would not go to Saul again, precisely because he loved him and could do nothing to reverse what God had decreed. The road that separates Ramah from Gibeah becomes, in the witnesses' reading, the visible shape of a deeper distance — the prophet and the king, once joined at the anointing, now sundered by the sentence of heaven, each going home to a solitude the other cannot ease.
35:
The chapter ends in mourning: "Samuel came no more to see Saul until the day of his death: nevertheless Samuel mourned for Saul; and the Lord repented that he had made Saul king over Israel." The witnesses first harmonize the statement with a later meeting. John Gill renders it "added not to see him" — "not that he saw him no more, he saw him afterwards," but that "he did not go to see him." Adam Clarke reconciles it with 1 Samuel 19:22–24, where Saul comes to Samuel at Naioth: "from this time Samuel had no connection with Saul; he never more acknowledged him as king; he mourned and prayed for him, and continued to perform his prophetic functions at Ramah," superintending "the school of the prophets." John Wesley makes the same distinction — Samuel came no more "to visit him, in token of respect or friendship, or to seek counsel from God for him"; the later meeting was "not Samuel that came thither with design to see Saul, but Saul went thither to see Samuel."

Keil and Delitzsch and Charles Ellicott weigh what the severance meant. Following Lange, Ellicott says "Saul's kingship, though still one de facto, yet from this time lost its theocratic relation. God's ambassador was recalled from him," and "the intercourse of the God of Israel with Saul through His Spirit came to an end, because Saul, sinking step by step away from God... had given up communion with God." Yet Ellicott dwells with real feeling on Samuel's grief: the old seer "had known Saul from the days of his splendid youthful promise" and "loved him much, and regarded him as his own adopted child." Saul "only lacked one thing: true faith in that God who loved Israel"; he "never really leaned on the Arm of the Mighty One of Jacob."

Matthew Henry gives the pastoral close the witnesses share. Samuel "mourned for Saul," thinking it "a very lamentable thing that a man who stood so fair for great things should ruin himself so foolishly," mourning too "for his everlasting state, having no hopes of bringing him to repentance." And Henry draws the abiding lesson: "we must mourn for the rejection of sinners," even "though we withdraw from them," and "though they do not mourn for themselves" — for "Saul seems unconcerned at the tokens of God's displeasure... and yet Samuel mourns day and night for him. Jerusalem was secure when Christ wept over it." So the chapter of a king's rejection ends not in triumph but in tears, the prophet grieving over the man God has set aside, and the reader left to feel the weight of a crown lost through the plain refusal to obey.
